## [06:01 - 09:02]

to use data and analytics. And rigorous doesn't necessarily mean hard. It means having structure and intention and a plan around it. So, what are our three principles, right? These are the most important things you can possibly do when you're working with data. Give your data a job. Use hypothesis testing to convert questions you have into marketing strategies. And then bringing context in to let you account for the unmeasurable. So, those are the three principles. Let's talk about what each of them means in a little more detail. First is give your data a job. Basically, this means that we need to make sure when we're collecting data in our business that data is going to be used to make a decision. If you're just pulling a lot of data into a database and you never look at it, you never use it, not only are you wasting opportunity in your business because you don't look at the data, but you're wasting your time because you're pulling a lot of metrics, which is usually somewhat time consuming, and then not looking at them. So, we always wanna make sure that we give our data a job, right? That each metric we're looking at in our business has some action that we take to make the metric look more like we want it to. You know, if your cost per lead is too high, you need to bring that down. And so, there are different ways you can do that by changing your opt-in page or changing the ads you're running. We're gonna get into the different jobs and actions that are tied to each metric, but you wanna make sure whatever information, whatever data about your business you're looking at, you know how that's gonna drive your behavior to make things better for your customers. Next is using hypothesis testing. So, iterative testing is the key to optimizing with data. What this means is when we are working with data in our business, we need to make small changes all the time. Maybe it's changing our ad copy, right? Our ad copy sounded too salesy, and so, we need to change it and put more of that personal story in there to increase the number of social likes and shares and comments or get people to engage. And doing that testing is kind of the basis of using data in our business. Testing doesn't always mean spending a lot of money to buy BWO, it's a website testing tool. It can just mean tweaking and changing your ad copy on Facebook. But what you wanna do is ask yourself questions about what you're seeing and use those questions to drive how you're changing stuff. ## [09:02 - 12:02]

You can step by step improve the overall flow from people seeing your business on Facebook to them opting in or buying something, becoming your customer. And then the last one, and the one that I don't think a lot of people really talk about or think about is trying to tie in context. It's so easy for us to forget that there's a world outside of our business that really affects our bottom line, that affects how our customers are living and engaging. So, you need to make sure you're remembering context when you're looking at data in your business. So, that would include the time of year, changes you make to your website, what's going on in your industry, things like that. I remember last December, I had a client who said, oh, okay, we ran this funnel back in August and we were getting lead costs of about $1.40, which they felt really good about. They said, okay, we can run this funnel at a profit. But I had to remind them, hey, it's December. This is a really, really busy time of year for paid traffic platforms. So, trying to use historical data to make a decision about what you think this campaign is gonna cost is not a good idea, because there's so much more inventory being bought up by big companies in preparation for Hanukkah and Christmas that your lead costs will go up at that time of year. So, by drawing in information like that, thinking about what's going on outside of our individual business, we can add a lot more color and context or data to help us actually make decisions. If you change something on your website and your performance plummets, it doesn't mean you need to delete all of your ads. The ads may still be working great. Instead, it's time to go look at your website and then fix it. Or a new competitor enters the space and your costs are going up because they're stealing your customers. ## [12:02 - 15:04]

they trust implicitly the numbers that you see in Facebook's ad platform. And I am not here to rag on Facebook at all. I think it has some really, really awesome reporting in there. But Facebook and Google's job is to get you as an advertiser to spend more money. They ultimately want you to say, boy, I wish I was spending $10,000 a day instead of $5 a day on Facebook. And so they have engineered the way that they track and credit sales to be very positive for them. And it's all about this kind of attribution idea, right? So attribution means how you decide who gets credit for when a sale or lead happens. And Facebook's default setting is a attribution of a one day view window and a seven day, I believe, click window. So if someone sees an ad in the newsfeed, they're just scrolling through, they don't even really stop and engage with it. And later that day, they go and buy from your website, Facebook is gonna take credit for that sale in the ads manager. So, what do you do about this? Well, by validating and verifying performance with a third party source, you can both rely on what you can from Facebook, but you can also make sure that Facebook is not over-crediting themselves with the sales. So tracking is really important, right? Because if Facebook counts everyone who sees your Facebook ads, but you run a big email campaign, your email is gonna do a lot more of the heavy lifting on actually driving those sales. So you might not be able to take what you see your cost per acquisition as and use that to project what you're gonna be able to spend next week. So we wanna make sure that we're trying to essentially confirm what Facebook has to say, prove that it is accurate and then use that information. So what are the benefits of tracking? Well, you can prove your value to your clients or your boss. So going in and showing like, hey, we used to spend $4 to acquire lead and now we're spending $2 to acquire lead because I launched all these ads that got really great social proof and we get way more shares now. And you have data, you have numbers that can show on this day I launched these new ad creatives and here we are a month later and our cost per lead is way down or our cost per acquisition is down. You can optimize based on data, not guesses. A lot of business owners sort of rely on that gut instinct, right? And the main thing we wanna do in ads manager is set up tracking to know when people click on our ads that when they get to our website, we can say, oh, they came from this ad, from this source, or this particular creative. And the way we do that is by adding UTM parameters. So this is, yeah, kind of a, it's a little ugly, I'll say it, but it's a helpful way to break it down. And so a UTM parameter, UTM stands for Urchin Tracking Module, something like that.

## [18:05 - 21:05]

It's basically a system that Google created that a lot of people have adapted and started to use as well that allows you to add details onto any hyperlink. So, HTTPS, www, coldandquill.com, right? This is Molly and I's friend and frequent collaborator Lindsey Marder, she works on this website. And so we have a URL. Normally when you're creating Facebook ads, you'll put the URL in, but we're gonna add some additional information so that we know when they hit our website exactly where they came from. So you wanna have a UTM source and that is your referring site. So that'll tell you where the traffic came from. So that could be Facebook or it could be Google. You could also include what email list you're mailing if you're not doing paid traffic specifically. Then we've got our referral type. So UTM medium for Facebook, that's gonna be CPC, cost per click. Sometimes I break this out into a cost per click and then retargeting. That's really a choice you can make. But if you're trying to keep it simple, CPC is a good one. UTM term, this is where we'll include the audience, right? So this will help describe who you are targeting with your ads. UTM content that tells you about the creative. It gives you information about what the ad is that you're using here, right? So maybe it's your video ad where you show a picture of the product being used or maybe it's your story-based ad. And then the last one is UTM campaign. And UTM campaign describes the big picture campaign that you are doing. And luckily for Facebook, it's already set up to use campaigns as it's sort of basic structure. So you already are familiar with this concept, right? So if I'm running, you know, Russ's modern publisher webinar, that would be the campaign name I would use here. The great news is Facebook has a lot of these values that can be input dynamically into your URL to track. ## [24:07 - 27:08]

So these are some of the most important metrics we can be tracking on our website. Page views is tracked natively in Google Analytics. As long as you have it on your site, that's gonna be tracked. This essentially describes what the visitor is doing on the website. And then there's the tag. And this tells Google Tag Manager what you wanted it to do whenever someone takes this action. So a good example of a trigger would be a page view. Somebody on your site views the thank you page, right? That might show you that they've made a purchase. There is one thing to keep in mind when you're looking at web analytics, and there's two kind of key ways to look at what's happening. You can either look at unique page views, so that's every time someone views a page, it tracks at the person level. Page views can't store that data,

is you don't get the same kind of customer journey aspect that you do with standard events, right? Awareness, when people learn that your company or brand exists, evaluation, when they are looking at or considering buying your product, and conversion, when they actually go ahead and make that choice to buy. Well, as an analyst, we have essentially the same structure, but we add one more step to it. But so we've got the top of funnel, so that's the awareness stage, right? That's when people kind of learn that your business exists. The middle of the funnel, this is when people maybe give you their email address, start to learn what products you have that are available, and then the bottom of funnel, where people actually make the purchase. And then we have the retention and monetization section, right? This is the circle at the bottom. And the reason we're talking about the funnel structure, there is a reason we didn't just totally change gears here, is because we need to make sure that we are thinking about metrics that affect each of these different stages. ## [36:12 - 39:14]

when the question is more, hey, I'm showing them this $40 product, did they go ahead and purchase or not? So we'll start at the top of funnel. And the most important thing at the top of the funnel is on platform engagement. So at the top of the funnel, it's before people necessarily click over to your website. This is where you wanna kind of understand how they're doing stuff on Facebook's platform that shows that they're interested in you. So the two big metrics here are cost per video view. And to calculate cost per video view, we divide the spend on whatever campaign it is we're looking at by how many three second video views there were. And this gives you a rough sense of how much do I pay to get someone to watch three seconds of one of my videos? That's a really good metric to sort of start building that relationship with cold traffic. Video views are an awesome way to get a lot of reach, to introduce your products, your brands, all the stuff that you do on Facebook for very cheap. The other big metric here at the top of the platform is cost per messenger signup. To calculate that one, it's also pretty easy, right? It's spend divided by the number of messenger signups. And with both of these, we are just looking at how much money we're spending, obviously, but then these actions people are taking inside of Facebook, which is why we're talking about on platform engagement here. And they give us a sense of how well are we using this sort of relationship in Facebook's court to bring people into the fold, right? To attract cold traffic to our business and our brand. So you calculate click through rate by dividing the unique link clicks by impressions. So you're dividing the number of people. and out of it 25 people clicked your ad, right? That's pretty good. That's a 2.5% click through rate, which is above average for most brands. That's a really helpful one because this one shows you how well your creative is driving people to want to learn more. Cost per click. So for this one, we're dividing the spin. And then the last one that's really relevant here at the middle for this section is relevant score. So we don't know how this is calculated. ## [42:16 - 45:17]

And then we've got the bottom of the funnel. So this is where the conversions happen. And really what we're tracking here is how well we are generating conversions. So metrics we want to look at are cost for acquisition. We divide the spend for our campaign by the number of customers it created. And revenue per visitor. Revenue per visitor is really, really useful metric. You're basically dividing how much money you make by the number of unique link clicks that come from that campaign. And I've got a really handy little kind of trick or hack when you're trying to be smart about your spend that we'll get to later. But revenue per visitor is something I know not a lot of people look at. I definitely think it's worth your time to track though. The other conversion tracking metrics we want to look at are return on investment. And I'm specifically talking about the immediate return on investment here. We get that by subtracting how much we spent on our ads from the revenue those ads generated, and then you divide that by the spend. And that shows you if I'm going to spend $100 on Facebook and make $200, you end up with 100 divided by 100. And you get 100%. So basically you've made double what you made. Return on investment is I think the most important thing you can possibly look at when you are budget conscious. Because this basically shows you if I put a dollar into Facebook, how many dollars am I gonna get out? Am I gonna get out 50 cents? Meaning I've got a negative ROI. Or am I gonna get it more than a dollar? Meaning I'm making money every time I buy ads. The other one, sales funnel performance. So I have a tracking sheet I'm gonna give you guys where you'll be able to record sales funnel performance. But you basically wanna track each step of your funnel. So people that visit the sales page or visit a product page, and then take an action, which would be either visiting the order form or adding a product to the cart. You wanna show how well people move from one step to the other. By looking at sales funnel performance, you can dig in and see what's working really, really well on your site and what's not working that well on your site. Places where people are getting stuck. Because then you can go in and improve what's going on on your website and unstick those people. One good thing or important thing I should say to mention when we're talking about conversion metrics, Facebook can natively track ROAS or return on ad spend. But ROAS and ROI are not the same metric. Return on ad spend is calculated by dividing spin by, or dividing revenue by spin. So when you get to 100% return on ad spend, you've made your money back. Return on investment, on the other hand, you're basically subtracting how much you've already invested in your advertising from your revenue. So a 0% ROI is break even. But you don't wanna think, oh, I'm shooting for 0%

## [45:17 - 48:19]

with ROAS and Facebook because you're gonna lose money that way. I really think ROI is a better metric in general to look at performance for your business because you're factoring in that cost upfront. When you get to 100% ROAS, it looks and feels a bit like you're making money, you're positive. But really, you've just made everything back. 0% ROI I think is more psychologically useful because you actually understand, if I'm losing money on this ad campaign, I'm in the negative. And if I'm over 0%, that helps me understand my actual return. And then the metrics in the last section, retention and monetization. So our goal here, kind of once people have already become customers is strategic growth. This is the opportunity to take people who have chosen to buy from your products and get them to buy again and again. And to keep following up with people who have made it through the funnel, maybe gotten cold. So here, I like to look at return on investment over time. So looking at the return on investment after seven days and after 30 days. And what's totally different about this is if you have any emails that go out to people that just became buyers or you have a follow-up system in place for your funnel, by looking at this return on investment over time, you're going to basically widen the range of time that you're including where people can be buying in a campaign. And it usually means that revenue goes up. But if you've got a product or service you're selling that takes a little bit of time to explain and talk through and get people to take an action, this is a really, really important way to look at and measure what's going on. Because if you only look at a immediate return, when you know it takes about seven days to sell your product, you're going to never see a Facebook campaign that performs like you want it to. So stretching out the period of time you're looking at the return on investment is really helpful. And then lead and customer value over time. So looking at the average revenue you make by first opt-in or first purchase can give you this. So you've got some lead magnet on your site. Once you've got that, you can look back over time and see how much money you make on average for people that get added to your CRM by taking that action. And when you know this, you can really smartly spend on the platform. Say you're selling a nail polish and you sell the nail polish, it basically break even. So $5 or whatever. you know that you can spend quite a bit more on that campaign to acquire customers. Because while you only make $5 upfront, over time they end up spending another $35. You can spend more money on the front because you know that over time those people will continue to buy and you'll make that money back. So it's a great way to sort of scale up, right? Are they leaving your website, right? You can find out where those sticky spots are and improve those. That's like where you know to focus your efforts. These are also influenced by your offer. ## [1:03:28 - 1:06:30]

Here's a couple of kind of hacks, simple ideas that make tracking hopefully a little bit easier to sort of think through. First one, you know you're good when your cost per click is less than or equal to your revenue per visitor. So once you get to these metrics being equal or where your revenue per visitor is higher, you basically have a winning campaign on your hands. There's all kinds of other aspects obviously, but you're essentially showing that every time somebody clicks, they become a visitor, right? So you know it costs a dollar for someone to click on the ad and if you make a dollar every time someone visits your site, you can do that ad infinitum, right? You can basically do that until the cows come home and you're going to at least break even if not start to make money. So keeping these two balanced, really, really great way to do a quick health check of your Facebook campaigns, especially purchase focused ones. Combining spend across related campaigns to better understand ROI. So this is a really, really important idea. And again, I would say this one is a little bit more advanced but it's useful. So when we think about our traffic system, right? The traffic plan that we built in session two, I think it was, there were multiple steps all working towards this end of getting people to buy something. And often we have a campaign on Facebook that's designed to sell a product but we also have a retargeting campaign, right? People that don't, that add to cart but don't buy, we're sending ads to them on Facebook saying, hey, you put this in your cart but you didn't purchase, just wanted to remind you about it. If you look at these two campaigns together, kind of combine their spend and combine the revenue that they generate, it can help you better understand your return on investment. A retargeting campaign for people who add to cart but don't buy is always gonna have super, super high ROI, return on investment because it's basically targeting the most excited, most active people who have not become buyers. The campaign that comes before that that sends people to the sales page, let's say, often will not have as good of a ROI, won't make as much money. But the retargeting campaign, sending people back to the cart, that one couldn't work without that first campaign driving people to the sales page. So these two have kind of a mutualistic relationship, right? And if you want to make better decisions and think smarter about your spending by looking at these two things together, you can understand ROI in a better way because neither of those campaigns can really exist without the other. A sales campaign may not make as much money and the retargeting campaign can't get people in it unless you've got a paid traffic campaign feeding it. And then my last little hacker trick, this doesn't work for everybody. Facebook has, wishy-washy done this a lot,

but there are custom audiences in Facebook and something that I really, really love to do that I think is super useful is to build more or less a quote unquote funnel with those custom audiences. ## [1:09:32 - 1:12:34]

this campaign, you know, could use some work, but leave it running, or green light, this campaign looks great. Like how do we scale it up? Those are just like really simple, boiled down ways to think about campaign performance. And an analyst can use all the metrics they're looking at to give more or less, you know, full stop, proceed with caution, or full steam ahead. Analysts work on long-term leave and customer evaluation. So something that I really believe every person needs to know about their business is what is a customer worth over time? What is a lead worth over time? Having an analyst is a great way to get that information, right, because their job is basically to look at over time how all of these customers go from a single buyer to a multi buyer, you know, what are people worth when they join your system over a year, over five years? Analysts are also supposed to do campaign tracking, Q and A. So they go through and look at your Facebook ads and make sure that everything has a UTM parameter. You definitely want to do this before launching. Nothing is more painful than realizing that there's no tracking on a campaign that's performing well out of the gate. So I definitely recommend having an analyst review, having an analyst do a QA on your campaigns to make sure that things look good. Analysts also do deep dive metric reviews. So when you're not just looking at sort of the surface metrics and you want to understand the big picture of the business, analysts are supposed to go in and look at that. Filter Google Analytics traffic to look at Facebook specifically. So when you are the analyst or, you know, when you're working with an analyst, having them go into Google Analytics and looking at funnel performance for Facebook, right, excluding whatever's going on with your email, whatever's going on elsewhere, is a really powerful and important step. You want to make sure that the analyst, whether it's you or someone else in your team, understands not just how the general population performs when they're on some part of your website, but how the Facebook visitors do that as well. As you also have your analysts do weekly check-ins for proven campaigns and daily check-ins for new campaigns and launches. You want to make sure you still do health checks to know that stuff looks good, but you don't need to be looking at it in depth every day. ## [1:12:34 - 1:15:37]

you want to be checking in every single day as an analyst, making sure that you know what's going on because it's a finite period of time for a launch or because the new campaign, you sort of make or break its life early on. increase over time as a campaign starts to fatigue and get old.
